zh.lifeSummary: 當Jane Masselin於1565年出生在英國維爾爾韓寧頓時，她的父親Henry Masselin25歲，母親Ann Edwarde25歲。她於大約1588年與Richard Franklin在英國英格蘭維爾爾結婚。他們至少是1個女兒的父母。她去世於1590年在英國英格蘭維爾爾享年25歲。

nameMeanings.name: Masselin
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Andre, Armand, Lucien, Germain, Jacques, Normand, Amedee, Fernand, Gilles, Laurent, Alphee, Benoit.
French: from an Old French personal name, a pet form of Asse; see Ace 1. Compare Ashline .
Altered form of French   Ancelin  . Compare   Ashland   3,   Ashley   2, and   Aslin   2.

variants.name: Ace
meanings: English (of Norman origin): from Old French Ace, Asse, or Asce, all from the ancient Germanic male personal name Az(z)o, Atso, Adso, or possibly the female personal name Aza. These were pet forms of ancient Germanic compound names formed with adal- ‘noble’, such as Adalbert (later Albert ) and Adalheidis (later Alice; see Allis ).

variants.name: Ashline
meanings: History: Bearers of the surname Ashline are descendants of one or another of the brothers Jacques and David Asselin from France (see Asselin ).

variants.name: Aslin
meanings: English: from the Middle English male personal name Asselyn (from Old French Acelin, Ascelin, from ancient Germanic Azilin). There was also a feminine form, A(s)celine.
Altered form of   Asselin   2, a surname of French origin.
Swedish (Åslin): topographic or ornamental name from ås ‘ridge’ + the suffix -lin, derived from Latin -(l)inius ‘relating to’. Compare Ashlin and Oslin .

variants.name: Ashley
meanings: English: habitational name from any of numerous places (especially in southern and central England) named Ashley, from Old English æsc ‘ash’ + lēah ‘woodland clearing’.
Americanized form of French Canadian   Asselin   2.
In some cases also an Americanized form of German   Eschler  .

nameMeanings.name: Jane
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Originally a feminine form of John , from the Old French form Je(h)anne. Since the 17th century it has proved the most popular of the feminine forms of John, ahead of Joan and Jean . It now also commonly occurs as the second element in combinations such as Sarah-Jane. In Britain it is still one of the most frequent of all girls' names. It is not a royal name, but was borne by the tragic Lady Jane Grey ( 1537–54 ), who was unwillingly proclaimed queen in 1553 , deposed nine days later, and executed the following year. Seventy years earlier, the name had come into prominence as that of Jane Shore , mistress of King Edward IV and subsequently of Thomas Grey , 1st Marquess of Dorset, Lady Jane's grandfather. Jane Shore's tribulations in 1483 at the hands of Richard III , Edward's brother and successor, became the subject of popular ballads and plays, which may well have increased the currency of the name in the 16th century. A 19th-century influence was its use as the name of the central character in Charlotte Brontë's novel Jane Eyre ( 1847 ). From 1932 to 1959 it was used as the name of a cheerful and scantily clad beauty whose adventures were chronicled in a strip cartoon in the Daily Mirror. It is also borne by the American film stars Jane Russell ( 1921–2011 ) and Jane Fonda ( b. 1937 ).

variants.name: Jayne
meanings: Elaborated spelling of   Jane  , a form popularized no doubt by the American actress  Jayne Mansfield  (  Vera Jayne Palmer ,  1933–67 ) and British ice-dancer  Jayne Torvill  (  b. 1957 ).

variants.name: Jain
meanings: Originally a feminine form of John , from the Old French form Je(h)anne. Since the 17th century it has proved the most popular of the feminine forms of John, ahead of Joan and Jean . It now also commonly occurs as the second element in combinations such as Sarah-Jane. In Britain it is still one of the most frequent of all girls' names. It is not a royal name, but was borne by the tragic Lady Jane Grey ( 1537–54 ), who was unwillingly proclaimed queen in 1553 , deposed nine days later, and executed the following year. Seventy years earlier, the name had come into prominence as that of Jane Shore , mistress of King Edward IV and subsequently of Thomas Grey , 1st Marquess of Dorset, Lady Jane's grandfather. Jane Shore's tribulations in 1483 at the hands of Richard III , Edward's brother and successor, became the subject of popular ballads and plays, which may well have increased the currency of the name in the 16th century. A 19th-century influence was its use as the name of the central character in Charlotte Brontë's novel Jane Eyre ( 1847 ). From 1932 to 1959 it was used as the name of a cheerful and scantily clad beauty whose adventures were chronicled in a strip cartoon in the Daily Mirror. It is also borne by the American film stars Jane Russell ( 1921–2011 ) and Jane Fonda ( b. 1937 ).

historicEvents.title: State Lottery
historicEvents.description: A State Lottery was recorded in 1569. The tickets were sold at St. Paul's Cathedral in London.

historicEvents.title: The Royal Exchange is Organized
historicEvents.description: The Royal Exchange was organized in January 1571 by Sir Thomas Gresham. Gresham is known as the father of English banking.

historicEvents.title: The First English Colony is Established in America
historicEvents.description: England established its first colony in North America in 1585 as a way to generate additional wealth. The colony was named Virginia, after the virgin Queen Elizabeth I.

historicEvents.title: Elizabeth I Signs Death Warrant for Mary, Queen of Scots
historicEvents.description: Elizabeth I signed the death warrant for Mary, Queen of Scots, on February 1, 1587, as she was found guilty of plotting to assasinate Elizabeth. Mary, Queen of Scots, was beheaded at Fotheringhay Castle in England on February 8, 1587.
historicEvents.urlTitle: Elizabeth I.org - Mary Queen of Scots

en.lifeSummary: When Jane Masselin was born in 1565, in Hannington, Wiltshire, England, her father, Henry Masselin, was 25 and her mother, Ann Edwarde, was 25. She married Richard Franklin about 1588, in Wiltshire, England, United Kingdom. They were the parents of at least 1 daughter. She died in 1590, in Wiltshire, England, United Kingdom, at the age of 25.
